questions.context:
Prescribed burning is a land-management practice in which fires are deliberately set under controlled conditions to reduce flammable vegetation. For much of the twentieth century, managers pursued the opposite goal, treating every wildfire as a threat to be extinguished as quickly as possible. [■] This policy of total suppression allowed dead branches, fallen leaves, and dense undergrowth to pile up across forest floors year after year.

These accumulations proved dangerous. [■] Deprived of the small, frequent fires that had once cleared them naturally, many forests built up enormous fuel loads that fed rare but catastrophic blazes. Ecologists gradually recognized that fire had always been part of these ecosystems and that removing it entirely disrupted the cycles on which certain plants depended. [■]

This insight reshaped modern practice. Controlled burns are conducted in cool, damp seasons, when flames stay low and predictable. [■] Such burns thin the underbrush, return nutrients to the soil, and open bare ground where fire-adapted seeds can sprout. However, prescribed burning still carries risk, since shifting winds may push a planned fire beyond its intended boundaries, and smoke can draw complaints from nearby residents.

questions.context:
The placebo effect is a phenomenon in which a patient's symptoms improve after receiving a treatment that contains no active therapeutic ingredient. [■] This response poses a serious problem for medical research, because it can make an ineffective drug appear to work. [■] When patients expect relief, their reported pain, mood, or fatigue often shifts even though nothing chemically meaningful has entered their bodies.

To separate genuine drug effects from mere expectation, researchers developed the randomized controlled trial. Participants are divided by chance into two groups, one receiving the real medicine and the other an identical-looking placebo. [■] Because neither the patients nor the doctors know who received which, an arrangement called double-blinding, expectations are spread evenly across both groups.

This method has become the standard for testing new medicines. Any improvement seen in the placebo group is subtracted from the treatment group, leaving the drug's true effect. [■] However, the approach has limits, since some treatments are difficult to disguise, and the placebo response itself may vary from one culture to another, so results that hold in one country may not repeat elsewhere.

questions.context:
A planetary atmosphere is the envelope of gases that a planet's gravity holds close to its surface. Mars presents a striking puzzle, because surface features that were carved by ancient rivers suggest that the planet once possessed a thick, warm atmosphere. Today its air is thin and cold, less than one percent as dense as Earth's. [■] Explaining how so substantial an envelope could nearly disappear became a central question in planetary science.

The leading explanation centers on the planet's magnetic field. Earth generates a strong global field that deflects the solar wind, a stream of charged particles flowing outward from the Sun. [■] Mars, by contrast, lost much of its internal heat early in its history, and its core solidified, shutting down the dynamo that had once sustained such a field. [■] Without this protective shield, the solar wind struck the upper atmosphere directly and stripped gas molecules into space over billions of years.

This model fits data from orbiting spacecraft, which still detect gas escaping from Mars today. [■] Yet the account remains incomplete, because researchers cannot fully reconstruct how quickly the loss occurred, and some of the missing gas may instead be locked within minerals beneath the surface.

questions.context:
Corrosion is the gradual deterioration of a metal caused by chemical reactions with substances in its surroundings. The most familiar example is rust, which forms when iron is exposed to oxygen and moisture over time. Early builders noticed that iron tools, bridges, and ships weakened and flaked as reddish-brown rust spread across their surfaces, yet they lacked any clear understanding of why the damage occurred.

This limited knowledge gradually gave way to practical remedies. Engineers learned to slow corrosion by coating iron with paint or grease, which blocked contact between the metal and the surrounding air. A more durable method, galvanization, covered steel with a thin layer of zinc that corroded in place of the iron beneath it. Such barriers extended the life of structures, though they often wore away over time and needed renewal.

This search for protection culminated in materials that resist corrosion from within. In the early twentieth century, metallurgists developed stainless steel by adding chromium, which forms an invisible, self-repairing oxide film on the surface. Cathodic protection, in which a more reactive metal is deliberately sacrificed, now guards pipelines and ship hulls. However, no method stops corrosion entirely, and metal structures still demand constant inspection and care.

options.D: long-lasting

questions.context:
The Silk Road was a network of overland routes that connected China with central Asia, India, and the Mediterranean world. Moving goods along it posed a formidable challenge, for the journey stretched thousands of kilometers across deserts, mountains, and lands governed by rival powers. No single trader could survive the entire distance, and the scarcity of water together with the constant threat of banditry made long stretches nearly impassable.

Merchants overcame these obstacles through a system of relays rather than continuous travel. Goods changed hands repeatedly, passing from one regional trader to the next, so that a bolt of silk was often traded through a dozen markets before reaching the West. Oasis towns grew into vital waystations where caravans rested and exchanged animals, and local rulers protected the routes in return for taxes on the passing trade.

This relay system carried far more than commodities. Religions, artistic styles, and technologies traveled the same paths and reshaped the societies they touched, while deadly diseases also spread along the crowded routes. The network gradually declined as sea routes proved cheaper and safer, though its long influence on cultural exchange endured. However, historians caution that the term 'Silk Road' oversimplifies a shifting web of connections that never followed a single fixed path.

options.D: The reasons that sea routes eventually replaced overland trade with China.

Oasis towns grew into vital waystations where caravans rested and exchanged animals, and local rulers protected the routes in return for taxes on the passing trade.

This relay system carried far more than commodities. Religions, artistic styles, and technologies traveled the same paths and reshaped the societies they touched, while deadly diseases also spread along the crowded routes. The network gradually declined as sea routes proved cheaper and safer, though its long influence on cultural exchange endured. However, historians caution that the term 'Silk Road' oversimplifies a shifting web of connections that never followed a single fixed path.

questions.subtopic: how the Doppler effect changes the pitch of a passing sound
questions.context: The Doppler effect explains something you have experienced many times without ever naming it. Picture an ambulance racing toward you with its siren blaring. As it approaches, the pitch sounds high and rising. The instant it passes and speeds away, the pitch drops noticeably lower. The siren itself never changes. What changes is your position relative to the source. Here is why. Sound travels as waves through the air, and a siren emits those waves at a steady frequency. When the source moves toward you, each successive wave is sent from a slightly closer point. The waves bunch up, arriving more frequently, and your ear interprets that higher frequency as a higher pitch. Once the source moves away, the opposite happens. Each wave leaves from a point farther off, the waves stretch out, and the pitch you hear falls. Notice that the speed of sound in the air stays constant the whole time. It is only the spacing of the waves reaching you that shifts. Now, engineers use this principle in ways that have nothing to do with sirens. A weather radar sends out a pulse and measures how the returning echo's frequency has shifted, which tells forecasters how fast rain is moving toward or away from the station. The same math underlies the radar guns police use to clock a speeding car. So the humble experience of a passing siren opens a door onto a serious scientific tool. And that brings us to light. Because light also travels in waves, it shows a Doppler shift too, and next time we will look at how astronomers read that shift to measure whether distant galaxies are rushing away from us.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why a sound's pitch changes as its source moves past a listener
options.B: How ambulance sirens are designed to warn nearby drivers
options.C: The different speeds at which sound and light travel
options.D: How police radar guns measure the speed of moving cars

questions.subtopic: the role of coffee houses in the European Enlightenment
questions.context: Coffee houses played a surprising role in the intellectual life of eighteenth-century Europe. When coffee first arrived from the Ottoman world, it was a novelty, but the establishments that served it quickly became something far larger than places to drink a warm beverage. In cities like London, Paris, and Vienna, coffee houses turned into hubs of conversation and debate. For the price of a single cup, usually a penny in London, anyone could enter, sit down, and join a discussion. That low price mattered enormously. Taverns served alcohol and often ended in rowdiness, but coffee houses were sober spaces where people came to talk, read newspapers, and trade ideas. Merchants, scientists, writers, and philosophers mixed in the very same rooms. In fact, some London coffee houses became so tied to particular groups that they functioned like early institutions. Lloyd's, which began as a coffee house, eventually grew into a famous insurance market. Others were gathering points for scientists who would later shape the Royal Society. Because such a wide range of people could meet and speak relatively freely, information and fresh arguments spread far faster than before. Ideas that might once have stayed within a small circle now reached a broad and curious public. Historians sometimes call these places penny universities, because for the cost of that single cup, a person could sit and listen to learned conversation and effectively receive an education. So the humble coffee house helped create the conditions for the Enlightenment. It gave the movement something it badly needed: an open, affordable place where reason and discussion could flourish among ordinary people, not just among the wealthy few.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How coffee was first imported into Europe from the Ottoman world
options.B: How coffee houses became important centers for exchanging ideas
options.C: How the London insurance market known as Lloyd's was founded
options.D: Why taverns declined in popularity during the eighteenth century

Merchants, scientists, writers, and philosophers mixed in the very same rooms. In fact, some London coffee houses became so tied to particular groups that they functioned like early institutions. Lloyd's, which began as a coffee house, eventually grew into a famous insurance market. Others were gathering points for scientists who would later shape the Royal Society. Because such a wide range of people could meet and speak relatively freely, information and fresh arguments spread far faster than before. Ideas that might once have stayed within a small circle now reached a broad and curious public. Historians sometimes call these places penny universities, because for the cost of that single cup, a person could sit and listen to learned conversation and effectively receive an education. So the humble coffee house helped create the conditions for the Enlightenment. It gave the movement something it badly needed: an open, affordable place where reason and discussion could flourish among ordinary people, not just among the wealthy few.

options.D: It forced philosophers to publish their ideas more cheaply

questions.subtopic: the process of pidgin languages developing into creoles
questions.context: Languages can be born in a single generation. That may sound impossible, but it is exactly what happens in the development of creole languages, and today I want to trace that process. It usually begins with a pidgin. A pidgin arises when groups of people who share no common language are suddenly forced to communicate, often through trade or, historically, on plantations. To get by, they cobble together a simplified system. A pidgin has a limited vocabulary, borrowed mostly from one dominant language, and a very basic, flexible grammar. Crucially, a pidgin is nobody's native language. It is a practical tool that adults use alongside their own mother tongues. Now here is where it gets interesting. When children grow up in a community where a pidgin is the main shared language, something remarkable happens. Those children do not simply learn the pidgin as it is. They expand it. They add consistent grammatical rules, a richer vocabulary, and the ability to express complex, abstract ideas. In doing so, they transform the pidgin into a full, complete language. That new language is a creole, and unlike a pidgin, it has native speakers. Linguists find this fascinating, because it suggests the human mind is not just passively absorbing language. Children appear to be actively imposing structure, filling in the gaps the pidgin left behind. Many researchers argue this reveals something deep about our built-in capacity for grammar. So a creole is not a broken or lesser language. It is a fully formed system, created in large part by children, out of the fragmentary raw material that their community handed them.

options.A: How trade between different groups created new vocabulary
options.B: Why children generally learn languages faster than adults do
options.C: How a simplified pidgin develops into a complete creole language
options.D: The differences between spoken and written forms of a language

questions.subtopic: biological magnification of toxins in food chains
questions.context: Consider a pond where a pesticide has been sprayed at a very low, seemingly harmless concentration. The water itself contains only tiny traces of the chemical. Yet the fish-eating birds at the top of that ecosystem can end up with enough of the toxin in their bodies to make them sick, or to keep their eggs from hatching. How does a harmless trace in the water become a dangerous dose in a bird? The answer is a process called biological magnification. It works through the food chain. The key is that certain toxins, like some pesticides and heavy metals such as mercury, do not break down easily, and they are not excreted from the body. Instead, they are stored, usually in fatty tissue. So start at the bottom. Microscopic organisms absorb a small amount of the toxin from the water. Small fish eat enormous numbers of these organisms, and the toxin from all of them builds up in each fish. Larger fish then eat many of those smaller fish, concentrating the toxin further. At every step up the chain, the poison becomes more concentrated in the animals' tissues. By the time you reach a top predator, the toxin can be many thousands of times more concentrated than it was in the water. The classic case is the pesticide DDT, which thinned the eggshells of birds like eagles and pushed some populations toward collapse. So a chemical that seems safe when you measure it in the water can be devastating at the top of the food chain. This is why scientists worry about even low levels of persistent pollutants entering an ecosystem.

options.A: How pesticides are sprayed onto ponds and nearby farmland
options.B: Why the pesticide DDT was eventually banned in many countries
options.C: How microscopic organisms absorb chemicals from pond water
options.D: How toxins grow more concentrated moving up a food chain

Notice: Spring Community Garden Plot Registration

The Redwood University Grounds Office is pleased to announce that registration for the spring community garden is now open. The garden plots are located behind Holloway Hall and will be assigned on a first-come, first-served basis.

Registration will be held in Room 118 of the Student Union from March 3 to March 14, between 10:00 AM and 3:00 PM. A refundable deposit of $20 is required to reserve a plot, and payment must be made by card at the front desk.

Please review the following guidelines:
- Each student may reserve one plot only.
- Hand tools are provided and must be returned to the garden shed after every visit.
- Plots that are left untended for three weeks will be reassigned to another gardener.

All new gardeners are expected to attend a short orientation on March 16. Questions may be directed to the Grounds Office at gardens@redwood.edu.
questions.stem: According to the notice, what is needed to reserve a garden plot?

options.A: A signed liability waiver form
options.B: A refundable deposit paid by card
options.C: A completed gardening experience survey
options.D: A note from a faculty advisor
